Name: information_theoretic_neural_pruning
Title: Information-Theoretic Neural Pruning: A Rate-Distortion Approach to Efficient Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: Neural networks can be efficiently pruned by leveraging information-theoretic principles, specifically rate-distortion theory, to identify and retain only the most informative weights and connections, resulting in a significant reduction in computational resources while maintaining predictive performance.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing, Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, and Geometric Signal Separation have explored the use of geometric and information-theoretic methods for efficient neural network inference. However, these approaches focus primarily on dynamic precision allocation, interference cancellation, and signal separation, whereas our proposal directly addresses the problem of neural pruning from an information-theoretic perspective, providing a more fundamental and principled approach to efficient neural network design.
Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic approach to neural pruning, leveraging rate-distortion theory to identify and retain only the most informative weights and connections in a neural network. Our method, termed Information-Theoretic Neural Pruning (ITNP), uses mutual information and entropy measures to quantify the importance of each weight and connection, and then prunes the network to meet a specified distortion constraint. We demonstrate the effectiveness of ITNP on several benchmark datasets, showing significant reductions in computational resources while maintaining predictive performance. Our approach provides a principled and efficient method for neural network pruning, with potential applications in a wide range of domains.
Experiments: Evaluate ITNP on benchmark datasets (e.g. CIFAR-10, ImageNet) using various neural network architectures (e.g. ResNet, DenseNet)
Compare ITNP to existing pruning methods (e.g. iterative magnitude pruning, lottery ticket hypothesis) in terms of computational resources and predictive performance
Investigate the effect of different distortion constraints and mutual information measures on the pruning process and resulting network performance
Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational cost of calculating mutual information and entropy measures may be high for large neural networks
The choice of distortion constraint and mutual information measure may significantly impact the pruning process and resulting network performance
ITNP may not be effective for neural networks with complex or non-linear relationships between weights and connections
